original_title: Inline gamma spectroscopy and liquid scintillation HPLC reveal daughters in (225)Ac-radiopharmaceutical.
title: Watching Ac-225's decay daughters show up in real time
blurb: Pairing inline gamma spectroscopy with liquid scintillation HPLC to actually catch the daughter nuclides riding along in an Ac-225 radiopharmaceutical. Those daughters are the whole reason alpha therapy dosimetry is a headache, so an analytical method that tracks them as they form is worth having even if it's not a fix.
blurb_long: Pairing inline gamma spectroscopy with liquid scintillation HPLC to actually catch the daughter nuclides riding along in an Ac-225 radiopharmaceutical. Those daughters are the whole reason alpha therapy dosimetry is a headache, so an analytical method that tracks them as they form is worth having even if it's not a fix. You get real-time resolution on Fr-221 and other decay products, which matters if you're trying to QC a manufactured batch or predict dose accurately. The tricky part is that you're still measuring a problem, not solving it—the daughters are going to be there either way, and their contribution to normal tissue dose doesn't go away because you can now see them clearly. But knowing what you have beats guessing, and if this holds up as a manufacturing tool it saves you from shipping material you can't properly dose.

original_title: Modular in vivo antibody–ADC click to reverse drug resistance in tumours
title: An antibody-drug conjugate you assemble after the antibody's already in the tumor
blurb: Click chemistry lets you attach a drug payload onto an already-administered therapeutic antibody in vivo, converting a plain antibody into an ADC in situ. Boosted antitumor activity against heterogeneous and treatment-resistant tumors, and the modular part means one payload chemistry could pair with antibodies already in the clinic instead of building a bespoke conjugate for each one.
blurb_long: Click chemistry lets you attach a drug payload onto an already-administered therapeutic antibody in vivo, converting a plain antibody into an ADC in situ. Boosted antitumor activity against heterogeneous and treatment-resistant tumors, though only in mice so far. The modularity is the real win: one payload chemistry could pair with antibodies already in the clinic instead of building a bespoke conjugate for each one. The real gamble is timing: the payload has to reach the tumor and ligate before the antibody has already cleared the tissue, and whether that window is real or just works in a mouse model is the open question. Off-target attachment in vivo also matters. If it works, it's a way to salvage antibodies that were borderline as naked agents and to iterate payloads without making a new ADC each time.

original_title: FcRn-Targeted Alpha Therapy Using [225Ac]Ac-Macropa-Rozanolixizumab Enables Potent Antitumor Activity
title: Repurposing an anti-FcRn antibody as an alpha-particle warhead
blurb: Rozanolixizumab, the anti-FcRn antibody used clinically to strip circulating IgG in autoimmune disease, gets fitted with a Macropa chelator and loaded with Actinium-225 instead, aimed at tumors rather than antibody clearance. FcRn's day job is recycling antibodies, not marking cancer cells, so it's an odd target for alpha therapy, but the paper reports potent antitumor activity.
blurb_long: Rozanolixizumab, the anti-FcRn antibody used clinically to strip circulating IgG in autoimmune disease, gets fitted with a Macropa chelator and loaded with Actinium-225 instead, aimed at tumors rather than antibody clearance. FcRn's day job is recycling antibodies, not marking cancer cells, so it's an odd target for alpha therapy, but the paper reports potent antitumor activity. The rationale bets on FcRn overexpression in the tumor vasculature or cancer microenvironment—plausible in some solid tumors but hardly assured. Ac-225's two-day half-life concentrates the dose locally, which matters because short-half-life alpha emitters are notoriously hard to deliver intact; keeping the radioactivity on target before decay is the whole advantage. Rozanolixizumab's binding affinity for FcRn was optimized for IgG stripping in circulation, not tumor targeting, so accumulation may hit an affinity ceiling if FcRn expression is borderline. Probably mice and early efficacy data at this stage, which means the numbers are only as robust as the tumor model and whether FcRn's expression pattern actually supports the hypothesis.

original_title: Side-by-Side Comparison of Different Thiol Bioconjugation Strategies for the Chemoselective Radiolabeling of Human Serum Albumin with Zirconium-89.
title: Maleimide finally gets a real head-to-head
blurb: Runs maleimide, PODS, and vinylketone side by side for Cys-specific labeling of human serum albumin with Zr-89, instead of the usual one-off comparison against maleimide as benchmark. Mal's thiosuccinimide linkage is known to leak in vivo, so this is basically an audition for what replaces it.
blurb_long: Runs maleimide, PODS, and vinylketone side by side for Cys-specific labeling of human serum albumin with Zr-89, instead of the usual one-off comparison against maleimide as benchmark. Mal's thiosuccinimide linkage is known to leak in vivo, so this is basically an audition for what replaces it. Zr-89 sees real clinical PET use, so a leaky linker there means a failed tracer, full stop. Running all three methods in parallel instead of one-against-maleimide benchmarking finally shows which ranks where. In vitro stability and labeling efficiency matter, but whether any of it translates to better in vivo retention is what actually counts.

original_title: Development of a High-Throughput Size-Exclusion Chromatography–Mass Spectrometry Workflow for Real-Time Monitoring of Bioconjugation of Cysteine-Based Antibody–Drug Conjugates
title: Real-time ADC bioconjugation monitoring with SEC-MS
blurb: A size-exclusion chromatography-mass spectrometry workflow that watches cysteine-based ADC conjugation as it happens, giving you drug-to-antibody ratios without waiting for end-point analytics. High-throughput means you can screen linker and payload combinations faster. Whether that's actually where your bottleneck is—conjugation rather than formulation, manufacturing, or animal testing—is another question.
blurb_long: A size-exclusion chromatography-mass spectrometry workflow that watches cysteine-based ADC conjugation as it happens, giving you drug-to-antibody ratios without waiting for end-point analytics. High-throughput monitoring means you can screen linker and payload combinations faster than endpoint assays and catch conjugation failures before they're baked in. For shops already iterating on DAR and linker chemistry, that feedback loop is useful, since you get direct readouts instead of waiting for QC analytics. But conjugation kinetics are one small piece of ADC development, and most timelines are constrained elsewhere: protein production, formulation stability, or animal testing. Still, if that's where you're stuck, the convenience is real.

original_title: Synthesis and in vivo evaluation of a bifunctional, glutamic acid derived chelator for the 203/212Pb theranostic pair
title: A lead chelator tuned by charge engineering
blurb: Glutamic acid-derived bifunctional chelator for Pb-203/212 theranostics, with charge optimization to improve pharmacokinetics. Charge engineering is a sensible design principle, though lead chelators are hardly in short supply.
blurb_long: Glutamic acid-derived bifunctional chelator for Pb-203/212 theranostics, with charge optimization to improve pharmacokinetics. Charge engineering is a sensible design principle, though lead chelators are hardly in short supply. The logic is straightforward: if a series of chelators can differ mainly in their net charge, you can optimize biodistribution for a given target without major structural redesign. But that only works if the different charges actually produce meaningful differences in vivo, and building up enough variants to optimize across is real work. Whether this approach solves a bottleneck in lead-212/-203 theranostics or mainly adds another option to a space that usually isn't constrained by chelator choice is what the data need to show.

original_title: Overall Survival with [177Lu]Lu-PSMA-617 Versus [177Lu]Lu-PSMA I&T: A Propensity Score-Matched Real-World Analysis
title: Lu-177 PSMA-617 and PSMA I&T, matched head to head on survival
blurb: A propensity-matched real-world comparison of the two Lu-177 PSMA ligands on overall survival, which is the question the registrational trials never ran directly against each other. Matching is not randomization and the usual real-world confounders apply, but it is the closest read we have on whether the ligand choice actually moves the outcome.
blurb_long: A propensity-matched real-world comparison of the two Lu-177 PSMA ligands — PSMA-617 and PSMA I&T — on overall survival, the head-to-head the registrational trials never actually ran. Matching is not randomization and the usual real-world confounders apply, but it is the closest read anyone has on whether the ligand choice moves the outcome rather than just the biodistribution. The interesting part is that these two agents get used almost interchangeably in practice, on supply and reimbursement grounds, as if the choice were cosmetic. If a survival gap holds up here, that assumption is wrong and the whatever-is-in-the-hot-cell-today approach needs rethinking. Treat the magnitude with suspicion — matched cohorts flatter whichever arm had the healthier patients — but the question it forces is the right one.
